currentSnapshot.definition: A person or animal with whom one spends time; a close associate or friend.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Broad modern use: a travel companion, a companion app, a companion animal. The word suggests togetherness more than deep intimacy — slightly more formal than 'friend'.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: She was his companion on the long journey north.

historicalSnapshots.definition: One who shares bread with another; a fellow at table.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: From Latin 'com-' (together) + 'panis' (bread). The word literally meant a bread-sharer — one who eats at the same table. This was a concrete act of social bonding: sharing food was the primary sign of trust and alliance.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: Compagnon: one who shares bread at the same table.

historicalSnapshots.definition: A person who associates with another; a fellow member of a group or guild.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The bread-sharing origin generalised to any close associate. Guild members were 'companions'; knights were 'companions' of orders. The word carried warmth and loyalty.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: He was a true companion in arms, never leaving the field.

historicalSnapshots.definition: An associate; one who keeps company with another; a fellow-traveller.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The word expanded beyond close bonds to general association. 'Companion' became a common term for travel, work, and circumstance — retaining warmth but becoming more neutral. Also used for paid companions in wealthy households.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: She engaged a companion to read to her in the evenings.

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From 'one who shares bread' to 'friend or associate' — a metaphor of physical sharing became the foundation of social bonding in language.
summaryOfChange.longSummary: The etymology of 'companion' reveals how literally the ancient world thought about friendship. To break bread together was the foundational act of alliance and trust — strangers became allies at the table. The Latin 'companio' encoded this concrete act: com- (together) + panis (bread). As the word passed through Old French into English, the bread-sharing became metaphorical, but the warmth of shared sustenance persisted. Today's companion retains the sense of togetherness and mutuality, even if no actual bread is involved. The word is a reminder that the language of friendship grew directly from the language of food.

keyDates.label: Latin 'companio' enters Old French
keyDates.significance: The Latin compound com+panis was the literal foundation of companionship: sharing a loaf of bread.

keyDates.label: Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
keyDates.significance: Chaucer's pilgrims are 'companions' in the original sense — fellow travellers sharing meals and the road.

keyDates.label: Broadening to general association
keyDates.significance: By the Early Modern period, 'companion' had expanded to any form of mutual company — the bread metaphor was forgotten but the warmth persisted.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — companion, n.
sources.attribution: Paraphrase and excerpt. OED content © Oxford University Press. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: From medieval Latin companio, companion-: one who eats bread with another, from com- together + panis bread.
sources.relevanceNote: The OED's etymology is unambiguous: this word is literally built from the act of sharing bread.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — companion, n. (modern senses)
sources.attribution: Paraphrase. OED content © Oxford University Press.
sources.excerpt: A person who associates with or accompanies another; a fellow-traveller; a comrade.
sources.relevanceNote: Shows the broadened modern sense — general association, not just table-sharing.

sources.title: Merriam-Webster — companion
sources.author: Editorial staff
sources.excerpt: one that accompanies another; a comrade, associate.
sources.relevanceNote: The fully broadened modern definition — togetherness without the bread.

sources.title: A Latin Dictionary
sources.author: Charlton T. Lewis, Charles Short
sources.excerpt: companio, -onis, m.: a messmate, fellow-soldier, companion (Vulg.)
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the original Latin sense: a mess-mate, literally one who shares rations.

relatedWorks.title: The Canterbury Tales
relatedWorks.creator: Geoffrey Chaucer
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Chaucer's pilgrims form a 'companye' — a group of companions in both the older 'bread-sharing' sense and the emerging 'travel group' sense. The word is central to the poem's social fabric.

relatedWorks.title: The Fellowship of the Ring
relatedWorks.creator: J.R.R. Tolkien
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: The 'Fellowship' and its companions self-consciously echo the medieval chivalric sense — Tolkien, as a philologist, was acutely aware of the word's history of shared bread and shared danger.

relatedPages.title: Semantic Drift
relatedPages.summary: The broad phenomenon of word meanings shifting across time.

relatedPages.title: Dictionaries vs Usage
relatedPages.summary: How dictionaries describe language — and why usage often leads the way.

relatedConcepts.label: lord
relatedConcepts.note: From Old English 'hlāford' (loaf-ward: bread-guardian). The lord was literally the person who guarded the bread supply.

relatedConcepts.label: lady
relatedConcepts.note: From Old English 'hlæfdige' (loaf-kneader). The lady was literally the person who kneaded the bread.

relatedConcepts.label: salary
relatedConcepts.note: From Latin 'salarium' (salt allowance). Another word where a basic food commodity is the origin of a social relationship.

ambiguityNotes: In computing, a 'companion app' or 'companion piece' uses the word in its broadest sense — supplementary or associated.
The 'paid companion' of Victorian households was a social role distinct from 'friend' — a class-inflected use that has since faded.
The word 'company' shares the same bread-root, so 'keeping company' preserves the original metaphor at one remove.

timelineEvents.title: Com + panis: the bread-sharer
timelineEvents.summary: Latin 'companio' was built from the act of eating together — sharing bread was the foundational act of alliance and trust.

timelineEvents.title: Chaucer's 'companye'
timelineEvents.summary: The Canterbury pilgrims formed a 'companye' — the word carried its full warmth of shared sustenance and shared road.

timelineEvents.title: Broadened to any association
timelineEvents.summary: 'Companion' expanded to any form of mutual company — travel, work, household employment. The bread-sharing metaphor faded but the warmth remained.

timelineEvents.title: Contemporary range
timelineEvents.summary: Today 'companion' spans close friendship, animal companionship, travel association, and product terminology — a fully broadened general-purpose word for togetherness.
